back_translation: The Pharisees and some scribes who came from the city of Jerusalem gathered together around Him.
And they saw some of His disciples eating food with defiled hands, that is, hands that had not been washed.
For the Pharisees and all the Jews strictly observe the tradition of the ancestors; they will not eat food until they have first washed their hands according to the ritual.

And when returning from the marketplace, they will not eat anything until they have first cleansed themselves; and there are many other traditions they have received to observe, namely the cleansing of cups, jugs, copper vessels, and dining couches.
The Pharisees and scribes asked Him: 'Why do your disciples not walk according to the tradition of the ancestors, but instead eat food with defiled hands?'
He answered them: 'Isaiah prophesied correctly concerning you hypocrites, as is written: This people honors me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me.
They worship me in vain, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men.'
You have abandoned the commandment of God and hold to the tradition of men instead.'
He said to them again: 'How clever you are at neglecting the commandment of God in order to keep your own tradition.
For Moses said: Honor your father and your mother; and: Whoever curses father or mother must be put to death.
But you teach that if anyone says to father or mother: Whatever benefit you might have received from me is Corban — that is, an offering dedicated to God —
you no longer permit him to do anything for his father or mother.
Thus you nullify the word of God by the tradition you have handed down, and you do many other such things.'
He called the crowd to come to Him again, and said to them: 'Everyone listen to me, and understand.
Nothing outside a person that enters into him can make him defiled; rather, it is what comes out of a person that defiles him.'
When He had entered the house, away from the crowd, His disciples asked Him about that parable.
He said to them: 'Are you also without understanding like that? Do you not understand that whatever is outside and enters into a person cannot defile him?
Because that does not enter into his heart, but into his stomach, and then is expelled into the latrine.' (By implication, He declared all kinds of food to be clean.)
He said further: 'It is what comes out of a person that defiles him.
For from within, that is from the heart of a person, come various evil thoughts: sexual immorality, theft, murder,
adultery, greed, wickedness, deceit, sensual desire, envy, slander, arrogance, and folly.
All these evil things come from within and defile a person.'
Jesus left from there and went to the region of the city of Tyre. He entered into a house, not wishing anyone to know, but He could not be hidden.
On the contrary, immediately a woman whose little daughter had an unclean spirit possessing her, hearing about Him, came and fell down at His feet.
This woman was a Greek, a Syrophoenician by birth. She pleaded with Him to cast the demon out of her daughter.
He said to her: 'Let the children eat their fill first, because it is not right to take the children's food and throw it to the puppies.'
She answered Him: 'True, Lord, but even the puppies under the table eat the children's crumbs.'
He said to her: 'Because of this answer, go your way; the demon has come out of your daughter.'
When she returned home, she found her child resting on the bed, and the demon had gone out.
Afterwards He left the region of the city of Tyre again, passed through the city of Sidon, and came to the Sea of Galilee, through the middle of the region of Decapolis.
People brought to Him a man who was deaf and had a speech impediment, and pleaded with Him to lay His hand on that person.
He took the man aside from the crowd to be alone, put His finger into his ears, spat, and touched his tongue.
He looked up to heaven, sighed deeply, and said to him: 'Ephphatha,' which means 'Be opened.'
Immediately his ears were opened, the chain of his tongue was loosed, and he spoke clearly.
He charged them not to tell anyone, but the more He charged them, the more widely they proclaimed it.
People were utterly amazed, and said: 'He has done all things excellently. He makes the deaf to hear, and the mute to speak.'
